name: States
classification_notes: A quintessential grotesque in the nineteenth-century tradition, with two subfamilies — Grotesque and Rounded — bridging historical poster and jobbing type traditions. The Pressure variable axis connecting both versions reinforces its grotesque lineage.
summary: A grotesque family in two versions — sharp and rounded — united by a variable Pressure axis that bridges historical poster display type with workhorse jobbing type.
description: States draws on the two poles of early sans-serif type: the crude, loud poster grotesque and the plain, functional small-print workhorse. Fatype synthesise these into 28 styles across States Grotesque and States Rounded, each running from Thin to Black with matching italics. A selection of alternates channels the idiosyncrasies of physical printing, and the variable font connects both subfamilies through a custom Pressure axis.
foundry_blurb: Fatype describe States as a synthesis of the two contrasting extremes of early sans-serif type — crude loud poster designs and plain no-frills jobbing type — released as States Grotesque and States Rounded, each in 14 styles. A selection of alternates turns the imperfections of physical printing into standout features, celebrating grotesque eccentricities that historically fell out of favour. The variable font includes a Pressure axis linking both versions, and is included in the Complete Family.

name: Beausite
classification_notes: Built on a neogrotesque skeleton with varying degrees of stroke contrast across four subfamilies, referencing the Art Deco high-contrast sans tradition while retaining neogrotesque structure.
summary: A four-subfamily type system that grafts graduated stroke contrast onto a neogrotesque skeleton, offering four distinct contrast levels from near-monotone to high-contrast Art Deco in feel.
description: Each of the four subfamilies — Classic, Fit, Slick and Grand — represents a different degree of stroke contrast, allowing designers to shift between moods while remaining within a coherent system. Classic runs the deepest range with ten weights plus italics, from Ultralight to Ultrablack, while Fit, Slick and Grand each offer six weights with italics. The Art Deco reference is worn lightly; this reads as a contemporary neogrotesque first, with contrast as a tuning mechanism rather than a period affectation.
foundry_blurb: Fatype describe Beausite as a typeface system that applies contrast onto a neogrotesque skeleton, offering four levels of contrast as an alternative within the neogrotesque category while revisiting the Art Deco trend for high-contrast sans in a contemporary fashion.

name: UCity Pro
classification_notes: UCity Pro is firmly within the geometric sans-serif tradition, explicitly described as evolving from the modernistic geometric style of Fatype's U8 typeface toward a more futuristic character through modern proportions and deliberate curve breaks.
summary: A geometric sans with deliberately broken curves and modern proportions, developed from Fatype's earlier U8 as a more digital and graphic interpretation of the genre.
description: Available in 10 styles across five weights from Light to Black, each with matching italics, UCity Pro builds on the geometric sans tradition by introducing selected curve breaks that give the letterforms a distinctly digital quality. A variable font covering both weight and slant axes is included with the complete family. Extended character support covers Cyrillic alongside Latin, and a wide set of OpenType features accompanies the release.
foundry_blurb: Fatype describe UCity Pro as an evolution of their U8 typeface, adapting the geometric sans-serif genre from its early modernistic origins into a more futuristic and graphic direction. Modern proportions and selected breaks in curves emphasise the digital character of UCity. The Pro version offers 10 styles, extended Cyrillic language support, broad OpenType functionality, and a variable font with weight and slant axes.

name: U8
classification_notes: U8 draws directly from early twentieth-century Berlin U-Bahn station lettering, sharing geometric reduction with DIN and Bauhaus-influenced sans-serifs; its stripped, rectilinear forms place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans drawn from the lettering of Berlin's U8 subway line signs, reviving an early twentieth-century modernist tradition that sits between the rigour of DIN and the ideals of the Bauhaus.
description: Anton Koovit based U8 on the station signage of the Berlin U-Bahn's U8 line, lettering that dates from roughly a century ago and connects the engineering clarity of DIN with Bauhaus geometric principles. The family spans fourteen styles across seven weights from Hairline to Black, each with a matching italic. It suits transport, editorial and identity work where a historically grounded geometric sans is needed without resorting to the more obvious mid-century revivals.
foundry_blurb: Fatype describe U8 as rooted in the lettering of Berlin subway station signs, particularly those of the U8 line. They characterise it as a decidedly modern design from about a century ago, emerging from a period of optimistic outlook towards the future. Designer Anton Koovit set out to restore what they call an undeservedly forgotten piece of design history, positioning U8 as a modernist classic bridging the engineers behind DIN and the ideals of the Bauhaus movement.

name: Laplace Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface explicitly derived from Walbaum's neoclassical Didone design, combining high-contrast letterforms characteristic of the Didone tradition with fixed-width spacing for digital use.
summary: A fixed-width interpretation of Walbaum's early 19th-century neoclassical design, translating Didone contrast and warmth into a contemporary monospaced format across six styles.
description: Drawing directly from Justus Erich Walbaum's German neoclassical typeface, it carries the high stroke contrast and refined character of Didone type into a fixed-width structure. Three weights from Light to Bold each include an italic, giving the family enough range for both running text and display use. The blend of metal-type warmth with digital precision makes it an unusual choice in a category where most designs favour neutrality.
foundry_blurb: Fatype describe Laplace Mono as a monospace that mixes the warmth of metal type with modern digital curves, presenting it as a contemporary take on Walbaum's famous German neoclassical design from the early 19th century.

name: Make Way
classification_notes: The foundry describes Make Way as a contemporary serif primarily inspired by Plantin, with a low contrast and solid appearance alongside transitional properties and triangular serifs — placing it clearly in the transitional serif category rather than purely Renaissance or old-style.
summary: A contemporary text serif rooted in Plantin, with triangular serifs, arched strokes and terminal drops that give the family a distinct texture at reading sizes.
description: Designed by Thibaud Tissot and initially developed for a book project at Onlab, Make Way takes Plantin as its starting point while introducing transitional rather than purely Renaissance characteristics. Triangular serifs combine with arched strokes and terminal drops, and OpenType stylistic sets include a schoolbook variant where the a, g and y switch forms, alongside more formal alternates for Q and R. Currently five weights with a single italic, the family sits within a broader Make family still in development.
foundry_blurb: Fatype describe Make Way as a colourful and lively contemporary serif, initiated for a book project by Onlab's Thibaud Tissot and later adapted for magazine use. Primarily inspired by Plantin, it retains a solid appearance with relatively low contrast suited to reading sizes, while triangular serifs, arched strokes and terminal drops mix round, straight, organic and geometric ingredients to give it a distinct texture. The full version includes OpenType alternates such as a schoolbook variant and more formal Q and R forms, and the foundry notes that additional italics and family members are planned.
